skill.name: networking-email-drafter
skill.description: Draft professional follow-up emails to contacts made at conferences. Generates polite, memorable messages with configurable tone and format options.
skill.summary: Generate professional conference follow-up emails with customizable tone and LinkedIn message support
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 45 static analyzer findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The 'external_commands' hits are markdown code fences (```bash, ```text) in SKILL.md documentation, not actual shell execution. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' hits are argparse initialization and YAML frontmatter text with no crypto code. The 'path traversal' hits are security checklist items warning against ../ usage. The 'hardcoded URL' is a legitimate GitHub source attribution link. The 'system reconnaissance' hits are documentation references to script file paths and audit JSON metadata. The actual code in scripts/main.py is a clean argparse-based email template generator with no network access, no file system operations, no dynamic code execution, and no data exfiltration. This skill is safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Markdown code fences flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected Ruby/shell backtick execution patterns at 32 locations across SKILL.md and references/audit-reference.md. All are false positives: these are markdown code fence markers (```bash, ```text, ```) used for documentation formatting, not actual shell command execution. The documented commands (python -m py_compile, python scripts/main.py --help) are standard Python development operations.

low_findings.title: Security checklist items flagged as path traversal
low_findings.description: Static analyzer detected path traversal patterns at SKILL.md lines 126 and 129. These are false positives: the lines are security checklist items that explicitly warn against path traversal ('No unauthorized file system access (../)' and 'Input file paths validated (no ../ traversal)'). They represent security guidance, not malicious code.

low_findings.title: Documentation URL flagged as hardcoded network endpoint
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a hardcoded URL at SKILL.md line 7. This is a false positive: the URL is a legitimate GitHub source attribution link pointing to the skill's own repository. It is documentation metadata, not an active network call.

low_findings.title: False positive weak cryptographic algorithm detections
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm at scripts/main.py:47 (argparse initialization), SKILL.md:3 (YAML frontmatter description), and SKILL.md:24 (feature description text). None of these locations contain cryptographic code. The skill does not implement any cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Documentation references flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged system reconnaissance patterns at multiple locations in SKILL.md and the audit result JSON. All are false positives: these are documentation references to file paths (e.g., 'scripts/main.py'), validation command suggestions, and JSON audit metadata. No actual reconnaissance behavior exists in any file.

content.value_statement: After a conference, writing follow-up emails to new contacts takes time and careful wording. This skill generates professional, memorable follow-up emails and LinkedIn messages with customizable tone, saving you effort while keeping your outreach personal and effective.
actual_capabilities: Generate professional follow-up emails from contact name, conference, and topic inputs
Support three tone options: formal, casual, and professional
Produce LinkedIn connection request messages as an alternative output format
Accept command-line arguments for batch or scripted email generation
Return consistent, well-structured email templates with greeting, body, and closing
Provide subject line suggestions and follow-up timeline guidance
limitations: Does not send emails; it only generates draft text for you to review and send manually
Email content is template-based and may need customization for highly specific situations
Limited to English language output only
Requires Python 3.10+ to run the packaged script

use_cases.title: Post-Conference Attendee Follow-Up
use_cases.description: A researcher attending a scientific conference wants to follow up with 10 new contacts they met. They run the skill with each contact's name, the conference name, and the discussion topic to generate personalized follow-up drafts quickly.

use_cases.title: LinkedIn Network Building
use_cases.description: A professional wants to connect on LinkedIn with people they met at an industry event. They use the LinkedIn message mode to generate short, friendly connection requests that reference their shared conversation.

use_cases.title: Event Organizer Thank-You Emails
use_cases.description: A conference organizer needs to send thank-you emails to speakers and panelists. They use the formal tone setting to generate respectful, appreciative follow-up messages that acknowledge each speaker's contribution.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Follow-Up Email
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a follow-up email to Dr. Sarah Chen. We met at the Neuroscience 2026 conference and discussed her research on synaptic plasticity. Use a professional tone.
prompt_templates.scenario: Simple one-contact follow-up with a specific research topic

prompt_templates.title: LinkedIn Connection Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a LinkedIn connection message for Michael Torres. We talked at the AI in Healthcare summit about machine learning for medical imaging. Keep it brief and friendly.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building professional network on LinkedIn after an event

prompt_templates.title: Formal Speaker Thank-You
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a formal thank-you email to Professor James Wright who gave a keynote at our Materials Science Symposium 2026. I want to express appreciation for his talk on sustainable polymers and mention that several attendees asked follow-up questions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Formal correspondence with a senior academic or keynote speaker

prompt_templates.title: Batch Follow-Up with Custom Tone
prompt_templates.prompt: I need follow-up emails for three contacts from the Genomics 2026 conference: Dr. Lisa Park (topic: CRISPR delivery vectors), Dr. Ahmed Hassan (topic: single-cell sequencing), and Dr. Rachel Kim (topic: epigenetics). Use warm tone for all, and include a request to schedule a virtual coffee chat.
prompt_templates.scenario: Multiple contacts requiring consistent tone and a specific call to action

output_examples.input: Contact: Dr. Maria Rodriguez, Conference: Cell Biology 2026, Topic: mitochondrial dynamics
output_examples.output:
Dear Dr. Rodriguez,

It was a pleasure meeting you at Cell Biology 2026. I particularly enjoyed our conversation about mitochondrial dynamics.

I would welcome the opportunity to stay in touch as we both work in this area.

Best regards,
[Your Name]

output_examples.input: Contact: James Wilson, Conference: AI Summit 2026, Topic: transformer architectures, LinkedIn mode
output_examples.output: Hi James, it was great meeting you at AI Summit 2026. I'd love to stay connected and continue our discussion about transformer architectures.

output_examples.input: Contact: Prof. David Kim, Conference: NeurIPS 2026, Topic: reinforcement learning, Tone: formal
output_examples.output:
Dear Dr. Kim,

It was a pleasure meeting you at NeurIPS 2026. I particularly enjoyed our conversation about reinforcement learning.

I would welcome the opportunity to stay in touch as we both work in this area.

Sincerely,
[Your Name]

best_practices: Review and personalize the generated draft before sending; add specific details from your conversation to make it genuine
Use the tone setting that matches your relationship: formal for senior academics, casual for peers, professional as the safe default
Run the script with --help first to confirm available options and validate your Python environment is ready
anti_patterns: Do not send the generated email without reviewing it; the template may need names, pronouns, or details adjusted for accuracy
Do not use the same generated template for every contact; customize each message with conversation-specific details to avoid appearing impersonal
Do not run the Python script with untrusted or unsanitized input from external sources without validation

faq.question: Does this skill send emails automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill only generates email draft text. You must copy the output and send it through your own email client. This keeps your email credentials secure and gives you full control over what is sent.

faq.question: What Python version do I need?
faq.answer: Python 3.10 or later is required. The script uses only the standard library (argparse), so no additional packages need to be installed.

faq.question: Can I generate messages for LinkedIn instead of email?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the --linkedin flag to produce a shorter LinkedIn connection request message instead of a full email. The LinkedIn message references your conference meeting and shared discussion topic.

faq.question: What tone options are available?
faq.answer: Three tones are supported: formal (uses titles and 'Sincerely'), casual (uses first names and 'Best'), and professional (the default, uses full names and 'Best regards'). Choose based on your relationship with the contact.

faq.question: Can I generate emails for multiple contacts at once?
faq.answer: The script handles one contact per run. To generate emails for multiple contacts, run the script once for each contact with their specific details. This ensures each message is properly personalized.

faq.question: Is my contact information stored or shared anywhere?
faq.answer: No. The script runs entirely on your local machine with no network access. Contact names, conference details, and generated emails are never sent anywhere or stored beyond your local workspace.
